Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic, but I made it work somehow. I'd usually carve out big chunks of time on weekends since my weekday schedule was unpredictable with work and other responsibilities. I relied heavily on reading and writing things out by hand, so I'd print materials when I could afford to or copy notes into notebooks. I tried to dedicate about 27 hours a week, but it wasn't always spread evenly—some weeks I'd barely manage, and others I'd cram everything in.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I couldn't afford the luxury of a perfectly consistent schedule because things always came up—picking up extra shifts, helping family, dealing with anxiety flare-ups. When deadlines loomed, I'd go into overdrive and study intensively for days. The rest of the time, I tried to chip away at readings when I could, but it was never as regular as I wished it could be.
Q3: Usually a deadline or an assignment reminder would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a discussion post or new material, and that would nudge me to check in. Honestly, I also logged in when my anxiety spiked about falling behind—I'd need to reassure myself about what was due and when. The gamified tasks actually helped because earning those little badges gave me a reason to check in even when nothing was technically due.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS became part of my morning routine, right after I woke up with my tea. The gamified elements helped with that—I liked seeing my progress and unlocking small achievements. But then life got overwhelming, and that routine slipped. I'd say it was most consistent in the first half of the semester before my anxiety really ramped up and attendance became harder to maintain.
Q5: The reading materials and written lecture notes were lifesavers for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I also found the discussion forums surprisingly helpful—seeing how other students interpreted things gave me new perspectives. The gamified tasks kept me motivated when everything else felt heavy; they broke down big goals into smaller, achievable pieces. Those little wins mattered more than people might think.
Q6: I tended to skip the videos or watch them at double speed without really absorbing much. Nearly 190 minutes sounds like a lot, but I often had them playing in the background while doing other things because I couldn't just sit and watch. Some of the optional supplementary readings I ignored completely—not because they weren't valuable, but because I had to prioritize what would directly affect my grade given my limited time and energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. I also valued anything that helped reduce my anxiety about the material—like practice quizzes or clear explanations. The gamified elements made even mundane tasks feel worthwhile because there was immediate feedback and a sense of accomplishment. If something felt like busywork with no clear purpose, I'd mentally check out.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford. Required activities had this weight to them—I knew I had to do them regardless of how I felt. Optional ones, I'd approach only if I had extra energy or if the gamification made them appealing. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping optional stuff, which added to my anxiety, but I had to be realistic about what I could handle.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, then study them on my phone during bus rides or print them out when I could. I filled notebooks with handwritten notes because writing helps me process information. I also used free resources online—YouTube explanations, open textbooks—because sometimes the course materials weren't clicking and I needed another perspective.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd study for hours from printed notes and my own handwritten summaries without touching the LMS at all. I'd work through problems on paper, reread highlighted passages in printed articles, and quiz myself using flashcards I made. None of that would show up anywhere in the system. My best studying often happened completely disconnected from the internet because it helped me focus without distractions.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a reading once, maybe skim it online to make sure it was the right file, and then never open it in the LMS again. I'd study that same document for days from my downloads folder or printed copy. The LMS would show one brief access, but that material might have been the foundation of hours of study. It was more practical for me given my internet situation isn't always reliable.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when my anxiety peaked. I'd feel paralyzed by how much I had to do and end up doing nothing, which only made things worse. Some weeks, sadness just settled over me and getting out of bed felt like an achievement, let alone logging into coursework. Financial stress also crept in—worrying about bills made it hard to focus on academics.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one—I'd be so exhausted from everything that I'd log in, stare at the screen, and retain nothing. Competing responsibilities constantly pulled me away; I don't have the luxury of being a full-time student with no other worries. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely sometimes because tackling them felt overwhelming. The workload across all 13 courses I was juggling was genuinely brutal.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd open readings and scroll through them without actually processing anything because my mind was elsewhere. Sometimes I'd click through modules just to mark them as complete, knowing I'd need to actually study them later from downloaded copies. My body was present but my brain was running through everything else I needed to handle that day.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they required me to be mentally present and articulate, which was hard when anxiety was high. I'd also avoid starting big assignments because the thought of them felt crushing, even though I knew waiting made it worse. Sometimes I'd tell myself I'd do something tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow would come with its own challenges. It's a cycle I'm still working on breaking.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd have quiet periods with minimal activity, then intense bursts right before things were due. The countdown to a deadline would spike my anxiety, which paradoxically sometimes motivated me and sometimes paralyzed me. I learned to use the gamified task system to create mini-deadlines for myself, which helped break the cycle a bit.
Q17: Feedback was really important to me, especially positive feedback—it helped counter my anxiety and self-doubt. When I got good marks on assignments, I'd feel motivated to engage more. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd spiral into worrying that I was doing everything wrong. The immediate feedback from gamified tasks was actually therapeutic; it gave me constant reassurance that I was on track.
Q18: The course structure was honestly a mixed bag. Having 13 online courses meant I was constantly juggling, and some courses were better organized than others. Clear weekly modules helped me stay oriented, but when things were scattered or the schedule shifted, my anxiety would spike. The gamified elements gave me structure when I needed it most—something concrete to work toward when everything else felt chaotic.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier—they gave me achievable goals and made progress visible. What made it harder was when materials weren't clearly labeled or when I couldn't tell what was essential versus supplementary. My low attendance rate wasn't because I didn't care; sometimes the timing of live sessions just didn't work with my life. More flexibility in how we could demonstrate engagement would have helped.
Q20: I think that could describe someone exploring and trying to understand the system, or someone who's anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work. It might also be someone who's struggling to focus—they want to engage but can't quite get traction. For me, high activity sometimes meant I was anxiously refreshing pages or re-reading instructions because I was scared of making mistakes.
Q21: That actually resonates with me quite a bit. My LMS engagement level was technically low, but I passed and my assignment scores were decent. I did a lot of my real learning offline—reading printed materials, writing notes by hand, working through concepts on paper. Someone looking at my login frequency wouldn't see the hours I spent with a highlighter and notebook. The LMS was just one tool, not my whole learning experience.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they click on, whether they're meeting deadlines. But they miss everything that happens offline, all the mental and emotional labor that goes into learning. They can't capture whether someone actually understood something or just clicked through it. They definitely can't show anxiety, sadness, or the effort it takes some of us just to show up.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm genuinely wrestling with the material, not just going through motions. It's when I'm asking questions, making connections, feeling curious even when it's hard. For me, engagement also means showing up despite anxiety, despite sadness, despite everything else pulling at me. It's not about being perfect or always visible—it's about caring enough to keep trying even when the system doesn't see your effort.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how many times I talked myself off an anxiety spiral to complete an assignment, or the hours I spent studying from handwritten notes at my kitchen table. It wouldn't capture the sadness I pushed through or how the gamified tasks genuinely helped my mental state by giving me small wins. It can't show resilience—how I kept going despite low resources, high anxiety, and a life that doesn't pause for coursework.
